name: Pony
summary: Sylvan Clebsch's Pony has six per-reference capabilities — iso, trn, ref, val, box, tag — that form a 3×3 matrix on (deny global aliases) × (deny local aliases). The sendable subset (iso, val, tag) is the diagonal: only those can cross actor boundaries. Static foundation for data-race freedom by construction, proven in Deny Capabilities for Safe, Fast Actors (Clebsch et al., AGERE 2015). consume and recover blocks let capability-changing assignments happen safely. The same mechanism doubles as object-capability authority — Pony is the cleanest example of one mechanism doing both ownership and ocap work.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Sylvan Clebsch's **Pony** has six per-reference capabilities — `iso` (read/write unique), `trn` (write-unique with `box` aliases allowed), `ref` (mutable, actor-local), `val` (deeply immutable, sendable), `box` (read-only view abstracting `ref`/`val`/`trn`), `tag` (identity-only, sendable) — that form a 3×3 matrix on (deny global aliases) × (deny local aliases).

The **sendable subset** is the diagonal (`iso`, `val`, `tag`): only those can cross actor boundaries because their local and global guarantees coincide. This is the static foundation for data-race freedom *by construction*, proven in "Deny Capabilities for Safe, Fast Actors" (Clebsch et al., AGERE 2015).

sources.label: Pony Deny Capabilities for Safe, Fast Actors

sections.title: consume and recover blocks
sections.content:
`consume` and `recover` blocks let capability-changing assignments happen safely:

- `consume` ends an alias to allow reassignment.
- `recover` lifts the capability of objects constructed inside it, so a freshly built `ref` graph can become `iso` if the recover block could only see sendable inputs.

These forms preserve the sendable-subset invariant while letting programs build mutable structures locally and then ship them across an actor boundary.

sections.title: The authority angle — capabilities as ocap
sections.content:
From the *authority* angle, Pony's six rcaps double as object capabilities. `tag` (opaque, identity-only) is exactly an actor reference under ocap discipline, and the sendable subset `{iso, val, tag}` is the static foundation for object-capability message passing — only sendable references cross actor boundaries, so data-race freedom and capability transfer fall out of the same type-system rule.

Pony is the cleanest example of one mechanism doing both ownership and ocap work: the same per-reference capability lattice that prevents data races also prevents ambient authority, because the only way to acquire a reference into another actor's heap is to be sent one.

sources.label: Pony Deny Capabilities for Safe, Fast Actors
